stem:
A term-born baby boy is 72 hours old and reviewed on the postnatal ward by the paediatric registrar after the midwife noticed visible jaundice extending to the trunk. He is breastfed every 2 to 3 hours, has had appropriate wet nappies and has lost 6% of birth weight. He was born at 39+2 weeks, the pregnancy was uncomplicated and the mother is O Rh positive.

The baby is alert, feeds vigorously, and has no organomegaly or pallor. There is no family history of haemolytic anaemia. His transcutaneous bilirubinometer reading is 285 micromol/L.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 144, RR 44, SpO2 99% room air, Temp 36.9
data_table.Examination: Alert, jaundice to mid-trunk, no hepatosplenomegaly, vigorous feeding
data_table.Investigations: Serum bilirubin 295 micromol/L (transcutaneous 285); DAT negative; blood group O+; FBC normal; reticulocytes normal

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Commence intensive phototherapy with a fibre-optic blanket or bank of lights and recheck serum bilirubin in 6 to 12 hours
options.rationale: Term infants at 72 hours with serum bilirubin around the phototherapy threshold on AU nomograms require intensive phototherapy and a repeat serum bilirubin within 6 to 12 hours to monitor response. (Source: RCH CPG: Neonatal Jaundice)

options.text: Recommend supplementary formula feeding to accelerate clearance and recheck bilirubin in 24 hours after observation
options.rationale: Routine formula supplementation is not first-line in breastfed infants; phototherapy is the intervention and continued breastfeeding is encouraged. (Source: RCH CPG: Neonatal Jaundice)

options.text: Discharge home with sunlight exposure advice and review with the general practitioner in 48 to 72 hours after discharge
options.rationale: Sunlight exposure is unsafe and ineffective; discharge near the phototherapy threshold without intervention risks acute bilirubin neurotoxicity. (Source: RCH CPG: Neonatal Jaundice)

options.text: Arrange exchange transfusion within 24 hours regardless of the rate of bilirubin rise and clinical response observed
options.rationale: Exchange transfusion is reserved for levels above the exchange threshold or signs of acute bilirubin encephalopathy; it is not first-line at this concentration. (Source: RCH CPG: Neonatal Jaundice)

options.text: Withhold any treatment and repeat serum bilirubin in 24 hours assuming physiological jaundice in a healthy infant
options.rationale: Physiological jaundice peaks at days 3 to 5 but levels near the phototherapy threshold mandate intervention and serial monitoring. (Source: RCH CPG: Neonatal Jaundice)

explanation.summary: Neonatal hyperbilirubinaemia is assessed against AU age-specific nomograms which plot serum bilirubin against postnatal hours; treatment lines are adjusted for gestational age and risk factors (haemolysis, sepsis, prematurity). Intensive phototherapy uses higher irradiance (>=30 microwatts per cm2 per nm) over a broad surface area and is monitored with repeat serum bilirubin at 6 to 12 hour intervals. Continued breastfeeding is encouraged; formula supplementation is reserved for inadequate feeding. Exchange transfusion remains the rescue therapy for severe hyperbilirubinaemia or acute bilirubin encephalopathy.
key_points: Use AU nomograms plotting bilirubin against postnatal age in hours
Intensive phototherapy is the first-line intervention at threshold
Continue breastfeeding during phototherapy
Repeat serum bilirubin every 6 to 12 hours during phototherapy
explanation.pearls: Serum bilirubin at threshold on the AU nomogram triggers intensive phototherapy plus repeat measurement at 6 to 12 hours; continue breastfeeding.

sources.label: NICE NG98 Jaundice in newborn babies

stem:
A previously well 5-month-old girl is brought to a regional emergency department by her parents with 24 hours of fever to 39 and one episode of vomiting. She is feeding 70% of normal and has decreased wet nappies overnight. She has no rash, no cough, no diarrhoea, no obvious focus on examination, and her ears, throat and chest are clear.

She has not received antibiotics recently. A clean catch urine collected by the midwife shows leucocyte esterase 3+ and nitrites 2+ on dipstick; microscopy shows pyuria. Blood cultures and urine culture have been sent.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 158, RR 42, SpO2 99% room air, Temp 39.1
data_table.Examination: Alert but irritable; no rash; soft abdomen; clear chest; ears normal
data_table.Investigations: Urine dipstick LE 3+ nitrites 2+; microscopy pyuria and gram-negative rods; WCC 19; CRP 75; blood culture pending

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial antibiotic regimen?

options.text: Oral cefalexin 25 mg per kg twice daily for 5 days with general practitioner review in 48 hours after discharge
options.rationale: Infants under 6 months with febrile UTI require intravenous antibiotic initiation in Australia until clinically stable and afebrile; oral monotherapy is unsafe at this age. (Source: RCH CPG: Urinary Tract Infection)

options.text: Admit for intravenous gentamicin and intravenous benzylpenicillin or ampicillin pending sensitivities and clinical response
options.rationale: Febrile UTI in infants under 6 months requires admission and intravenous therapy; AU first-line is gentamicin plus benzylpenicillin or ampicillin pending culture and sensitivity. (Source: RCH CPG: Urinary Tract Infection)

options.text: Intravenous ceftriaxone monotherapy for 7 days irrespective of culture results once initial blood culture is drawn
options.rationale: Ceftriaxone is reserved when first-line cover is inadequate or sepsis is severe; gentamicin and penicillin remain first-line for uncomplicated febrile UTI in young infants. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Oral trimethoprim for 7 days with renal ultrasound at 6 weeks if symptoms persist beyond 5 days of therapy
options.rationale: Trimethoprim has significant resistance and is not first-line for febrile UTI in young infants; the AU pathway requires IV therapy at this age. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Oral nitrofurantoin for 5 days, encouraging increased fluid intake and follow-up urine culture in 1 week
options.rationale: Nitrofurantoin does not achieve adequate tissue levels for pyelonephritis; it is reserved for older children with uncomplicated cystitis. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

explanation.summary: Infants under 6 months with febrile urinary tract infection require admission for intravenous antibiotics in Australia due to the risk of bacteraemia and renal scarring. First-line cover is gentamicin (single daily dose) plus benzylpenicillin or ampicillin, transitioning to a narrow-spectrum oral agent once afebrile and sensitivities known, for a total course of 7 to 10 days. A renal tract ultrasound is recommended after the first febrile UTI under 6 months; further imaging (MCUG, DMSA) depends on the ultrasound findings and clinical course.
key_points: Febrile UTI under 6 months: admit for IV gentamicin + benzylpenicillin/ampicillin
Ultrasound urinary tract after first febrile UTI in this age group
Switch to oral once afebrile and sensitivities known
Beware enteric gram-negative resistance to trimethoprim
explanation.pearls: AU first-line for febrile UTI under 6 months: IV gentamicin + benzylpenicillin/ampicillin then oral switch on sensitivities for a 7 to 10 day total course.

stem: A 3-month-old boy with bronchiolitis admitted 18 hours ago to a metropolitan paediatric ward has deteriorated. He has been on high-flow nasal cannula at 2 L per kg per minute with FiO2 0.4 for 6 hours. Despite this his work of breathing has increased with marked subcostal and intercostal recession; he is now only intermittently rousable. His latest capillary blood gas shows pH 7.20, pCO2 9.1 kPa, HCO3 22 mmol/L, lactate 2.4 mmol/L. He is being suctioned hourly and has stopped feeding orally. The treating registrar phones the local paediatric intensive care service.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 188, RR 72, SpO2 92% on HFNC FiO2 0.4, Temp 37.6
data_table.Examination: Marked subcostal and intercostal recession; only intermittently rousable; sluggish capillary refill 3s
data_table.Investigations: Capillary blood gas pH 7.20 pCO2 9.1 kPa HCO3 22 lactate 2.4; chest X-ray shows hyperinflation with peri-hilar streaking

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Increase HFNC flow to 3 L per kg per minute and continue current FiO2 with reassessment in 60 minutes before any other escalation
options.rationale: HFNC has clearly failed with rising CO2 and altered mental state; further uptitration delays definitive ventilatory support. (Source: ANZICS Paediatric Respiratory Support Guidance)

options.text: Trial nebulised salbutamol 2.5 mg back-to-back for 30 minutes before any further respiratory support escalation step
options.rationale: Bronchodilators have no place in bronchiolitis and do not address the central failure of ventilation in this scenario. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Prepare for intubation and arrange paediatric intensive care retrieval team transfer to a tertiary paediatric ICU
options.rationale: Bronchiolitis with rising CO2, reduced mental state and HFNC failure has an indication for intubation and PICU transfer; AU paediatric retrieval services should be engaged early. (Source: ANZICS Paediatric Respiratory Support Guidance)

options.text: Commence non-invasive bilevel ventilation locally and continue oral feeds while awaiting tertiary paediatric ICU advice
options.rationale: BiPAP can be a bridge in some centres, but with reducing GCS and ongoing rise in CO2 a controlled intubation is preferred before deterioration. (Source: ANZICS Paediatric Respiratory Support Guidance)

options.text: Administer intravenous methylprednisolone 1 mg per kg and start broad-spectrum antibiotics empirically for bacterial superinfection
options.rationale: Steroids do not modify bronchiolitis and empirical antibiotics are not indicated without a clear focus; the immediate problem is ventilatory failure. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

explanation.summary: Severe bronchiolitis in infants is the commonest reason for paediatric ICU admission with viral illness. AU practice tiers respiratory support from oxygen via nasal prongs, to HFNC, to non-invasive ventilation (in selected sites), to intubation; HFNC failure markers include rising CO2, reducing GCS, increasing work of breathing, and tachycardia. Once HFNC failure is recognised, the treating team should escalate to PICU rather than uptitrate flow indefinitely. The paediatric retrieval service should be contacted early to facilitate inter-hospital transfer.
key_points: HFNC failure markers: rising CO2, reducing GCS, persistent tachypnoea and tachycardia
Escalation pathway is to NIV or intubation, not higher HFNC
Engage paediatric retrieval services early for inter-hospital transfer
Bronchodilators, steroids, and antibiotics have no role even in severe bronchiolitis
explanation.pearls: Rising pCO2 above 8 kPa with reducing GCS on HFNC in bronchiolitis is an indication for intubation and PICU transfer, not further HFNC uptitration.

subtopic_detail: Mandatory reporting of suspected child abuse
stem:
A 9-month-old boy presents with a 24-hour history of right thigh swelling. His mother reports that he fell from a low couch. He is not yet walking or pulling to stand and has had no previous fractures. On examination, there is bruising over the thigh, mild swelling, and pain on movement. Imaging shows a spiral fracture of the right femoral shaft.

The infant also has a 2 cm bruise on the left buttock and a torn frenulum. There is no other history offered, no medical illness, and the mother appears anxious but engaged. The treating intern is unsure what to do next.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 138, BP 90/56, RR 32, SpO2 99% room air, Temp 36.8
data_table.Examination: Thigh swelling, bruising over thigh and buttock, torn frenulum, no other injuries on initial inspection
data_table.Investigations: Plain radiograph right femur: spiral fracture mid-shaft; FBC, coags, LFTs normal

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Reassure the mother that accidental fractures are common in this age group and discharge with orthopaedic follow-up
options.rationale: Spiral femoral fractures in non-mobile infants are highly suspicious for inflicted injury; discharge without further safeguarding assessment is unsafe. (Source: RCH CPG: Child Abuse)

options.text: Request a metabolic bone disease workup before initiating any safeguarding referral or specialist child protection assessment
options.rationale: Metabolic workup may be considered later but should not delay child protection processes; the clinical picture mandates immediate safeguarding action. (Source: RCH CPG: Child Abuse)

options.text: Discuss the fracture with the family for further explanation and consider safeguarding only if no plausible account emerges
options.rationale: The injury pattern in a non-mobile infant warrants immediate mandatory reporting and specialist child protection involvement, irrespective of subsequent family account. (Source: State Mandatory Reporting Legislation)

options.text: Notify child protection as mandated reporter and admit for skeletal survey, ophthalmology fundoscopy and child protection team consult
options.rationale: Spiral femur fracture in a non-mobile infant with additional bruising and torn frenulum is highly suspicious for non-accidental injury; the doctor is a mandatory reporter, the child requires admission with skeletal survey, ophthalmology fundoscopy review and child protection consultation. (Source: RCH CPG: Child Abuse; State Mandatory Reporting Legislation)

options.text: Apply a thomas splint, admit under orthopaedics for fracture management and arrange social work follow-up at discharge
options.rationale: Orthopaedic management alone misses the child protection imperative; admission must include safeguarding processes including skeletal survey and statutory notification. (Source: RCH CPG: Child Abuse)

explanation.summary: Doctors in all Australian states and territories are mandatory reporters of suspected child abuse. Spiral or metaphyseal fractures, posterior rib fractures, multiple fractures of different ages, torn frenulum in non-mobile infants, and bruising in non-mobile infants are sentinel injuries that warrant immediate child protection involvement. Workup includes skeletal survey, head imaging (CT or MRI), ophthalmology fundoscopy, baseline coagulation studies, and consultation with the local child protection team. The threshold to report is reasonable suspicion, not proof.
key_points: Spiral femoral fracture in a non-mobile infant is a sentinel injury
Mandatory reporting threshold is reasonable suspicion, not proof
Workup: skeletal survey, head imaging, fundoscopy, coags
Specialist child protection team should be consulted early
explanation.pearls: Sentinel injuries (bruise/torn frenulum/spiral fracture in a non-mobile infant) trigger mandatory reporting AND a structured medical safeguarding workup.

stem:
A 3-week-old male infant is recalled to a tertiary paediatric centre after a positive newborn screening test for cystic fibrosis. He was born at term, was breastfed within the first hour and is now feeding well with appropriate weight gain. He has no respiratory symptoms, no steatorrhoea and no failure to thrive at this point. His parents are not known to each other to be carriers.

Genetic testing on the newborn screening dried blood spot shows two CFTR mutations: F508del and a class V splice-site mutation. The pulmonary nurse phones the on-call paediatric registrar.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 138, RR 42, SpO2 99% room air, Temp 36.8
data_table.Examination: Alert, feeding well, no respiratory distress, no abdominal distension, normal anal sphincter tone
data_table.Investigations: Immunoreactive trypsinogen elevated x2 screens; CFTR genotype F508del and class V splice-site mutation

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in confirming and initiating management?

options.text: Reassure parents that compound heterozygous mutations are usually mild and review with the general practitioner in 6 months
options.rationale: Confirmation of CFTR dysfunction is required regardless of mutation class; deferring to 6 months is unsafe and not consistent with the AU pathway. (Source: Royal Childrens Hospital CF Pathway)

options.text: Arrange faecal elastase only and start pancreatic enzyme replacement therapy empirically without further confirmatory testing
options.rationale: PERT is initiated after demonstrating pancreatic insufficiency through faecal elastase plus clinical assessment; it is not the only step and does not confirm CF. (Source: Royal Childrens Hospital CF Pathway)

options.text: Refer to the geneticist alone for counselling about carrier risk and defer all CF treatment until symptoms emerge later
options.rationale: Genetic counselling is essential but presymptomatic CF management is the AU standard; deferring care worsens outcomes. (Source: Royal Childrens Hospital CF Pathway)

options.text: Reassure that two mutations meet diagnostic criteria; commence azithromycin prophylaxis and salt supplementation immediately
options.rationale: Two CFTR mutations support but do not yet replace a sweat test in the AU pathway; azithromycin prophylaxis is not initiated at 3 weeks. (Source: Royal Childrens Hospital CF Pathway)

options.text: Refer to the tertiary paediatric CF service for confirmatory sweat chloride testing, formal multidisciplinary review and structured surveillance
options.rationale: Positive newborn screening with two CFTR mutations is confirmed by sweat chloride testing in an accredited CF centre, followed by tertiary multidisciplinary review including respiratory, gastroenterology, dietetics, physiotherapy, social work and genetic counselling. (Source: Royal Childrens Hospital CF Pathway)

explanation.summary: Newborn bloodspot screening for cystic fibrosis in Australia uses elevated immunoreactive trypsinogen followed by reflex CFTR genotyping; positive screens are referred to a tertiary CF centre for confirmatory sweat chloride testing. Multidisciplinary care begins in the neonatal period with respiratory clearance education, nutritional review, pancreatic insufficiency screening (faecal elastase), and CFTR modulator eligibility assessment. The diagnosis is confirmed when sweat chloride exceeds 60 mmol per L; values of 30 to 59 prompt repeat testing and ongoing follow-up. Newborn diagnosis improves nutritional and pulmonary outcomes substantially.
key_points: Sweat chloride >60 mmol/L confirms CF in symptomatic and screen-positive infants
Two CFTR mutations support but do not replace sweat test confirmation
Tertiary multidisciplinary care starts in the neonatal period
Faecal elastase guides need for pancreatic enzyme replacement therapy
explanation.pearls: Sweat chloride above 60 mmol/L confirms CF diagnosis in the AU newborn screening pathway, with multidisciplinary tertiary care from the neonatal period.

stem:
A 5-year-old girl is brought to a general practitioner by her mother with 6 weeks of progressive patchy hair loss on the scalp. The child attends a primary school where several other children have similar findings. On examination there is a 3 by 4 cm area of broken hair shafts, mild scaling, and a slightly tender posterior occipital lymph node.

There is no kerion formation, no honey-coloured crusting, and no widespread inflammation. She is otherwise well, fully vaccinated and growing along the 50th centile. She has no known immunodeficiency or recent antibiotic use.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 96, RR 22, SpO2 99% room air, Temp 36.6
data_table.Examination: Patchy alopecia 3 by 4 cm with broken hairs and fine scale; tender occipital lymph node; no kerion
data_table.Investigations: Mycology skin scraping and plucked hair sample sent; KOH preparation pending

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial treatment?

options.text: Commence oral griseofulvin or terbinafine and add an antifungal shampoo to reduce household spread, awaiting mycology
options.rationale: Tinea capitis requires systemic antifungal therapy (oral griseofulvin or terbinafine) for adequate hair-shaft penetration; an antifungal shampoo (selenium sulfide or ketoconazole) reduces spore shedding and household spread. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Dermatology)

options.text: Apply topical clotrimazole cream twice daily for 6 weeks and recommend avoiding sharing brushes and pillows in the household
options.rationale: Topical antifungals alone do not penetrate the hair shaft and are inadequate as monotherapy for tinea capitis. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Dermatology)

options.text: Prescribe oral cefalexin for 10 days for likely bacterial superinfection of the scalp before any antifungal therapy
options.rationale: There is no evidence of impetiginisation; antibiotics do not treat dermatophyte infection and delay definitive antifungal therapy. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Dermatology)

options.text: Recommend short-course oral prednisolone alongside topical betamethasone to suppress inflammation as initial measures
options.rationale: Steroids may worsen tinea capitis (tinea incognito) by suppressing inflammation without treating the dermatophyte. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Dermatology)

options.text: Reassure the family the condition is self-limiting and review in 8 weeks with no specific treatment beyond emollient
options.rationale: Untreated tinea capitis progresses to scarring alopecia and continues to transmit; treatment is required to prevent permanent hair loss. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Dermatology)

explanation.summary: Tinea capitis in children is typically caused by Trichophyton or Microsporum species and presents with patchy alopecia, scaling, broken hair shafts and posterior cervical lymphadenopathy. Diagnosis is clinical, supported by mycology of skin scraping and plucked hairs (more sensitive than scrape alone). Systemic therapy is required for hair-shaft penetration: oral griseofulvin or terbinafine for 4 to 8 weeks. Adjunctive antifungal shampoo reduces spore shedding and household transmission. Untreated disease may produce kerion (boggy inflammatory mass) or scarring alopecia.
key_points: Topical antifungals alone are inadequate for tinea capitis
Oral griseofulvin or terbinafine for 4 to 8 weeks is the AU first-line
Antifungal shampoo (selenium sulfide or ketoconazole) reduces spread
Steroids alone risk tinea incognito
explanation.pearls: Tinea capitis requires oral griseofulvin or terbinafine for 4 to 8 weeks plus antifungal shampoo; topical alone is inadequate for hair-shaft penetration.

stem:
An 8-year-old girl is brought by her parents to a general practitioner with 6 weeks of progressively worsening headaches. The headaches initially occurred once a week and now occur daily. They are worse in the morning and on coughing or straining, and she has vomited (without nausea) on three mornings this week. She has been clumsier than usual on the netball court.

There is no fever, no neck stiffness and no rash. On examination she has a mild right-sided lower facial weakness and dysmetria of the right hand. Her family history includes migraine in her mother.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 88, BP 108/68, RR 18, SpO2 99% room air, Temp 36.7
data_table.Examination: Mild right facial weakness; right dysmetria; gait slightly wide-based; fundi reveal blurred disc margins

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step?

options.text: Commence amitriptyline 10 mg nightly as migraine prophylaxis and review at the general practice clinic in 4 weeks
options.rationale: Daily morning headaches with vomiting, focal neurology and papilloedema are red flags incompatible with primary headache; starting prophylaxis without imaging is unsafe. (Source: RCH CPG: Headache)

options.text: Refer urgently for same-day paediatric assessment with neuroimaging (preferably MRI) to evaluate for intracranial pathology
options.rationale: Progressive morning headache with vomiting, focal neurological deficit and papilloedema indicates raised intracranial pressure; AU paediatric guidance is urgent same-day specialist review and neuroimaging, preferring MRI over CT in stable patients. (Source: RCH CPG: Headache)

options.text: Order an outpatient MRI brain and review the imaging in 7 to 10 days as next available radiology appointment
options.rationale: Outpatient delays of a week are unsafe for raised intracranial pressure red flags; same-day assessment is required. (Source: RCH CPG: Headache)

options.text: Trial sumatriptan nasal spray during the next acute episode and review in the migraine clinic over coming weeks
options.rationale: Triptans are inappropriate when red flags suggest secondary headache; they neither diagnose nor treat the underlying lesion. (Source: RCH CPG: Headache)

options.text: Arrange lumbar puncture today before any imaging to measure opening pressure and exclude meningitis or pseudotumour cerebri
options.rationale: Lumbar puncture before imaging is contraindicated in suspected mass lesion due to coning risk; imaging precedes LP when red flags are present. (Source: RCH CPG: Headache)

explanation.summary: Paediatric headache red flags that mandate urgent neuroimaging include progressive course, morning headache with vomiting, change in personality or school performance, focal neurological deficits, papilloedema, headache worsened by Valsalva, and headache in children under 3 years. MRI is preferred in stable patients due to better posterior fossa visualisation and no radiation; CT is reserved for acute deterioration or when MRI is unavailable. Lumbar puncture is deferred until imaging excludes a mass lesion.
key_points: Morning headache + vomiting + focal signs + papilloedema = same-day imaging
MRI is preferred unless acutely unstable or MRI unavailable
Avoid LP until imaging excludes a mass lesion
Outpatient imaging delays are unsafe in red-flag paediatric headache
explanation.pearls: RCH paediatric headache red flags: morning headache, vomiting, focal signs and papilloedema mandate same-day specialist review and imaging.

stem:
A 32-year-old non-pregnant woman attends her general practice clinic with 36 hours of dysuria, urinary frequency, and suprapubic discomfort. She has no fever, no flank pain, no nausea and no vaginal discharge. She has no history of antibiotic use in the past 6 months and no recent travel. She is otherwise well, sexually active in a stable relationship, and uses combined oral contraception.

A urine dipstick in the surgery is positive for nitrites and leucocyte esterase. There is no history of recurrent UTIs and she has normal renal function.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 80, BP 118/72, RR 14, SpO2 99% room air, Temp 36.7
data_table.Examination: Mild suprapubic tenderness, no flank tenderness, no signs of systemic illness
data_table.Investigations: Urine dipstick positive for nitrites and leucocyte esterase; urine MCS not routinely required

lead_in: What is the most appropriate empirical antibiotic regimen?

options.text: Oral ciprofloxacin 500 mg twice daily for 3 days targeting common urinary tract gram-negative pathogen profiles
options.rationale: Fluoroquinolones are reserved due to resistance and tendinopathy risk; they are not first-line for uncomplicated UTI in Australia. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Oral amoxicillin clavulanate 500 mg three times daily for 5 days as a broad-spectrum first-line agent for cystitis
options.rationale: Amoxicillin clavulanate is broader than necessary and has higher community resistance than the recommended first-line agents. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Oral trimethoprim 300 mg daily for 3 days or nitrofurantoin 100 mg twice daily for 5 days as first-line therapy
options.rationale: AU first-line therapy for uncomplicated cystitis in non-pregnant women is trimethoprim 300 mg daily for 3 days or nitrofurantoin 100 mg twice daily for 5 days; both are narrow-spectrum and have low resistance rates. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Oral cefalexin 500 mg twice daily for 5 days based on patient preference and reported convenient twice daily dosing
options.rationale: Cefalexin is a second-line agent due to higher resistance and ecological impact; it is not the AU first-line option. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Oral fosfomycin 3 g as a single dose plus phenazopyridine 200 mg three times daily for symptomatic relief over 48 hours
options.rationale: Fosfomycin is an alternative for resistant organisms; phenazopyridine is not available on PBS in Australia and is not recommended routinely. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

explanation.summary: Uncomplicated UTI in non-pregnant women is diagnosed clinically supported by urine dipstick; routine urine culture is not required unless symptoms recur or fail to respond. AU first-line treatment is trimethoprim 300 mg daily for 3 days or nitrofurantoin 100 mg twice daily for 5 days; both have favourable resistance and ecological profiles. Pyelonephritis (fever, flank pain, vomiting) requires broader spectrum cover and longer duration. Recurrent UTI (more than 2 in 6 months) warrants behavioural advice, self-start regimens or post-coital prophylaxis depending on triggers.
key_points: Trimethoprim 300 mg for 3 days or nitrofurantoin 100 mg BD for 5 days is first-line
Urine MCS not routinely required for uncomplicated cystitis
Pyelonephritis needs broader spectrum cover and longer course
Quinolones and broad-spectrum agents are not first-line in Australia
explanation.pearls: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic first-line for uncomplicated UTI: trimethoprim 300 mg for 3 days or nitrofurantoin 100 mg BD for 5 days.

stem:
A 72-year-old man with stable congestive heart failure and type 2 diabetes presents to his general practitioner with 36 hours of fever, cough, myalgia and fatigue. There is high influenza activity in the community. He has no chest pain, no haemoptysis, no significant shortness of breath, and his observations are stable. He is fully vaccinated this season. His chest examination is clear.

Nasopharyngeal swab PCR confirms influenza A. He has not been hospitalised this year and lives independently with his wife. His current medications include perindopril, carvedilol, frusemide, metformin and atorvastatin.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 92, BP 132/82, RR 18, SpO2 96% room air, Temp 38.4
data_table.Examination: Mildly fatigued; chest clear; no peripheral oedema; no signs of respiratory distress
data_table.Investigations: Nasopharyngeal swab PCR positive for influenza A; chest examination clear

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management decision?

options.text: Reassure, recommend rest and oral fluids, and review only if symptoms worsen significantly over coming days
options.rationale: Symptomatic management alone misses an opportunity to shorten symptom duration and reduce complication risk in a high-risk older adult with comorbidities. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic; AU Influenza Treatment Guidance)

options.text: Prescribe oral amoxicillin 500 mg three times daily for 5 days for likely bacterial co-infection given his comorbidities
options.rationale: Antibiotics are reserved for documented or strongly suspected bacterial pneumonia, not routine influenza without focal signs. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Prescribe inhaled zanamivir 10 mg twice daily for 5 days as the preferred neuraminidase inhibitor for older adults
options.rationale: Inhaled zanamivir is contraindicated in patients with chronic respiratory disease and is less practical for elderly patients; oral oseltamivir is preferred. (Source: AMH; Therapeutic Guidelines)

options.text: Prescribe oral oseltamivir 75 mg twice daily for 5 days starting today as treatment within 48 hours of symptom onset
options.rationale: Oral oseltamivir within 48 hours of symptom onset shortens illness and reduces complications in high-risk groups (age over 65, comorbidities); 75 mg twice daily for 5 days is the AU standard dose. (Source: AMH; Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Recommend baloxavir marboxil single oral dose for first-line treatment in older adults with influenza A confirmed on PCR
options.rationale: Baloxavir is not currently first-line in Australia and access is limited; oseltamivir remains the AU standard. (Source: AMH)

explanation.summary: Oral oseltamivir is the AU first-line antiviral for influenza, most effective when started within 48 hours of symptom onset. Indications include severe or progressive illness, hospital admission, and high-risk groups: age over 65, chronic cardiac, respiratory, renal, hepatic, neurological, or metabolic disease, immunosuppression, pregnancy and postpartum, Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander adults, and morbid obesity. Vaccinated status does not preclude treatment in confirmed infection. Antibiotics are reserved for documented bacterial co-infection (otitis media, sinusitis, pneumonia).
key_points: Oseltamivir 75 mg BD for 5 days is the AU first-line antiviral
Start within 48 hours of symptom onset for maximal benefit
High-risk groups include age >65, chronic disease, pregnancy, Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander adults
Vaccination does not preclude treatment
explanation.pearls: Oseltamivir 75 mg BD for 5 days within 48 hours of symptom onset is the AU standard for influenza in older or comorbid adults.

stem: A 24-year-old tall thin male university student presents to the emergency department with 4 hours of sudden right-sided pleuritic chest pain and mild dyspnoea while sitting in a lecture. He has no past medical history, does not smoke, and takes no medications. He is haemodynamically stable, breathing comfortably and able to speak in full sentences. His chest radiograph shows a right apical pneumothorax measuring 3 cm from the chest wall at the level of the hilum. The remainder of the chest is unremarkable. There is no tension physiology and no underlying lung disease evident.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 90, BP 122/78, RR 18, SpO2 96% room air, Temp 36.7
data_table.Examination: Reduced breath sounds right apex, mildly hyperresonant percussion right side, trachea midline
data_table.Investigations: Chest radiograph right apical pneumothorax 3 cm at hilum; no underlying parenchymal disease

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Insert a 28 French intercostal catheter immediately for definitive drainage and admit under cardiothoracic surgery for observation
options.rationale: Large-bore intercostal catheters are not first-line for primary spontaneous pneumothorax in stable patients; smaller-bore approaches or aspiration are preferred. (Source: TSANZ/BTS Pneumothorax Guideline)

options.text: Reassure, discharge with simple analgesia and outpatient respiratory review in 4 weeks without any acute intervention
options.rationale: A 3 cm pneumothorax exceeds the AU threshold for conservative management at home; intervention is required as outpatient. (Source: TSANZ/BTS Pneumothorax Guideline)

options.text: Perform needle thoracostomy at the second intercostal space midclavicular line emergently before any definitive imaging
options.rationale: Needle thoracostomy is reserved for tension pneumothorax with haemodynamic compromise; he is stable. (Source: TSANZ/BTS Pneumothorax Guideline)

options.text: Commence non-invasive positive pressure ventilation to accelerate re-expansion of the visceral pleural defect noted today
options.rationale: Positive pressure ventilation worsens pneumothorax and is contraindicated; it does not accelerate sealing of the visceral pleural defect. (Source: TSANZ/BTS Pneumothorax Guideline)

options.text: Perform needle aspiration with a 16G catheter and reassess radiologically; if unsuccessful insert a small-bore intercostal drain
options.rationale: AU and BTS guidance for primary spontaneous pneumothorax with size above 2 cm in a stable patient is initial needle aspiration with a small-bore catheter; if aspiration fails (residual size above 2 cm) a small-bore intercostal drain is inserted. (Source: TSANZ/BTS Pneumothorax Guideline)

explanation.summary: Primary spontaneous pneumothorax classically affects tall thin young men and is graded radiologically by size (apex to cupola or chest wall at hilum), with 2 cm a common threshold. AU and BTS guidance support initial needle aspiration over routine large-bore chest drainage for primary spontaneous pneumothorax above 2 cm in stable patients; small-bore intercostal drains are second-line if aspiration fails. Large-bore drains are not first-line. Tension pneumothorax requires immediate needle decompression followed by intercostal drainage. Recurrence prompts surgical bullectomy plus pleurodesis.
key_points: Primary spontaneous pneumothorax above 2 cm in stable patient: needle aspiration first
Small-bore intercostal drain follows failed aspiration
Tension pneumothorax = immediate needle decompression then drainage
Positive pressure ventilation is contraindicated until pneumothorax drained
explanation.pearls: Stable primary spontaneous pneumothorax >2 cm in size is managed first with needle aspiration; small-bore drain follows if residual size remains >2 cm.
